introduction: This artifact captures the structured requirements for Phase 1 of af-hub: a service that collects telemetry and audit data from agent-fox (AF) instances and enables external users to self-onboard via OAuth and authenticate AF instances via time-bound API keys. Requirements are derived from the accepted PRD and cover bootstrap initialization, user management, authentication & OAuth onboarding, API key management, RBAC, API design, CLI behaviour, and security constraints.

glossary.0600: POSIX file permission mode granting read and write access to the file owner only.
glossary.0700: POSIX directory permission mode granting read, write, and execute access to the directory owner only.
glossary.DRI: Directly Responsible Individual; the person or team accountable for a deliverable.
glossary.OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant: The RFC 8628 OAuth flow used by the af-hub CLI to authenticate users in headless/terminal environments without a local HTTP server or browser redirect.
glossary.POSIX: A family of standards for Unix-like operating systems; referenced in the context of file permission modes (e.g. 0600, 0700).
glossary.RBAC: Role-Based Access Control; the permission model used by af-hub to gate access to API operations.
glossary.TLS: Transport Layer Security; the cryptographic protocol required to secure all af-hub API traffic in production deployments.
glossary.af-hub: The central hub service that collects telemetry and audit data from running agent-fox instances and serves as the management plane for those instances.
glossary.agent-fox: The AF application instances (running on VPSs or in Kubernetes pods) that report data to af-hub.
glossary.argon2: A memory-hard password-hashing algorithm used as an alternative to bcrypt for storing API key hashes in af-hub.
glossary.auto-merge: The process by which af-hub automatically links a new OAuth provider login to an existing account when the email address matches an existing record, preventing duplicate accounts.
glossary.base64url: URL-safe Base64 encoding (RFC 4648 §5) without padding characters, used to encode plaintext API keys for presentation to users.
glossary.bcrypt: A password-hashing algorithm used to store API key hashes in af-hub.
glossary.bearer token: An opaque credential presented in the HTTP Authorization header (Authorization: Bearer <token>) to authenticate API requests.
glossary.bootstrap token: The long-lived API key generated on first initialization of af-hub that acts as the root credential for initial admin setup.
glossary.conflict: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 409 when the request conflicts with existing state.
glossary.created_at: Provenance field on database records storing the UTC timestamp at which the record was created.
glossary.created_by: Provenance field on database records storing the user ID of the principal who created the record.
glossary.device_code: A short-lived opaque code issued by the device authorization endpoint that the CLI polls against until the user completes browser-based authorization (RFC 8628).
glossary.editor: The single RBAC role defined in Phase 1; grants full read/write access to all resources scoped to a specific repo.
glossary.entropy: The degree of randomness in a generated secret; af-hub requires a minimum of 256 bits (32 random bytes) of cryptographically secure entropy for API keys.
glossary.expires_at: The timestamp field on an API key or bootstrap token that marks the point after which the credential is no longer valid.
glossary.internal_error: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 500 when an unexpected server-side error occurs.
glossary.key_id: The unique identifier for an API key record in af-hub.
glossary.key_not_found: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 404 when the referenced API key does not exist.
glossary.oauth_provider: The external identity provider used for OAuth authentication; supported values in Phase 1 are github, gitlab, google, and keycloak.
glossary.permission_denied: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 403 when the authenticated principal lacks the required role.
glossary.provider_account_id: The account identifier issued by an OAuth provider for a specific user account.
glossary.repo: A Git repository (on GitHub, GitLab, or another Git forge) that is under management by agent-fox; the fundamental tenant boundary in af-hub.
glossary.repo_id: The unique identifier for a repo record in the af-hub database; all data rows are keyed by this value.
glossary.repo_not_found: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 404 when the referenced repo does not exist or is not accessible.
glossary.session token: The opaque token returned to the CLI after successful OAuth device authorization, persisted to disk and used for subsequent authenticated API calls.
glossary.tenant: The unit of multi-tenancy in af-hub; a repo is the tenant boundary in Phase 1.
glossary.token file: The plain-text file on disk in which the CLI persists the session token after a successful login or signup.
glossary.token_expired: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 401 when a bearer token or API key has passed its expires_at timestamp.
glossary.token_invalid: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 401 when a bearer token or API key is malformed, unrecognised, or revoked.
glossary.user_code: A short, human-readable code displayed by the CLI that the user enters in the browser during the OAuth Device Authorization Grant flow.
glossary.user_id: The unique identifier for a user record in af-hub.
glossary.user_not_found: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 404 when the referenced user does not exist.
glossary.validation_error: Machine-readable error code returned with HTTP 422 when a request body or parameters fail schema validation.
glossary.verification_uri: The URL printed by the CLI that the user visits in a browser to complete the OAuth Device Authorization Grant flow.

user_story.goal: have an admin user and a bootstrap token automatically created on first initialization
user_story.benefit: so that I can perform initial setup without any prior credentials

acceptance_criteria.action: creates an admin user and a long-lived `bootstrap token` on first initialization
prints the plaintext `bootstrap token` to stdout exactly once during initialization and never stores it in plaintext
stores the `bootstrap token` as a `bcrypt` or `argon2` hash only; subsequent retrieval of the plaintext is not possible
applies a configurable expiry to the `bootstrap token` with a default of 90 days from initialization time

edge_cases.action: must not re-run the initialization routine or overwrite the existing admin user and `bootstrap token`

user_story.goal: rotate the bootstrap token via a dedicated endpoint
user_story.benefit: so that I can revoke a compromised or expired root credential and obtain a new one immediately

acceptance_criteria.action: issues a new `bootstrap token`, immediately invalidates the previous token with no grace period, and returns the new plaintext token in the response body exactly once
acceptance_criteria.return_contract: HTTP 200 with the new plaintext `bootstrap token` in the response body; the token is never retrievable again after this response
acceptance_criteria.trigger: an admin calls `POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate` with a valid admin token

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the rotation request with HTTP 401 and code `token_invalid` or `token_expired`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: the caller presents an invalid or expired token to `POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate`

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the rotation request with HTTP 403 and code `permission_denied`

edge_cases.action: must ensure the previously valid `bootstrap token` is immediately rejected with code `token_invalid` on any subsequent request
edge_cases.error_condition: the old plaintext `bootstrap token` is presented to any endpoint

user_story.goal: create and modify user records via the API
user_story.benefit: so that I can manage users who cannot or have not yet completed the OAuth self-onboarding flow

acceptance_criteria.action: creates a user record containing `username`, `email`, `oauth_provider`, `provider_account_id`, `created_at`, and `created_by` fields and returns HTTP 201
acceptance_criteria.return_contract: HTTP 201 with the created user record
acceptance_criteria.trigger: an admin calls `POST /v1/users` with a valid admin token and a well-formed request body

acceptance_criteria.action: applies the requested modifications to the user record and returns HTTP 200
acceptance_criteria.return_contract: HTTP 200 with the updated user record
acceptance_criteria.trigger: an admin calls `PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}` with a valid admin token and a well-formed request body

acceptance_criteria.action: stores `repo_id`, `created_by`, and `created_at` provenance fields on every user record
must reject the request with HTTP 422 and code `validation_error`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: `POST /v1/users` is called with a body that omits `username` or `email`

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the request with HTTP 422 and code `validation_error`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: `POST /v1/users` is called with `oauth_provider` set but `provider_account_id` absent, or vice versa

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the request with HTTP 409 and code `conflict`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: `POST /v1/users` is called with an `email` that already exists in the system

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the request with HTTP 403 and code `permission_denied`
must return HTTP 404 and code `user_not_found`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: `PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}` is called with a `user_id` that does not exist

edge_cases.action: links the new `oauth_provider` to the existing account without creating a duplicate user record
edge_cases.trigger: a `POST /v1/users` request is submitted with an `email` matching an existing account but a different `oauth_provider`

user_story.goal: self-onboard via OAuth without admin intervention
user_story.benefit: so that I can start using af-hub immediately after authenticating with my existing GitHub, GitLab, Google, or Keycloak account

acceptance_criteria.action: supports OAuth authentication exclusively via GitHub, GitLab, Google, and Keycloak as identity providers in Phase 1
automatically provisions a new user account and returns a `session token`
acceptance_criteria.trigger: a user completes the OAuth flow for the first time using an email address not already in the system

acceptance_criteria.action: resumes the existing session and returns a `session token` without creating a duplicate account
acceptance_criteria.trigger: a user completes the OAuth flow using an email address already in the system

acceptance_criteria.action: automatically links the new `oauth_provider` to the existing account (`auto-merge`) without creating a duplicate user record
acceptance_criteria.trigger: a user authenticates via a second `oauth_provider` using an email address that matches an existing account

acceptance_criteria.action: requests at minimum the `email` and `profile` OAuth scopes (or provider equivalent) to obtain a verified email address during OAuth authorization

edge_cases.action: must reject the OAuth flow and return an appropriate error if the provider does not supply a verified email address
edge_cases.error_condition: an OAuth provider returns a response without a verified email address

user_story.goal: authenticate via the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant in a terminal environment
user_story.benefit: so that I can sign up or log in without needing a local HTTP server or browser redirect

acceptance_criteria.action: calls the af-hub device authorization endpoint and receives a `device_code`, `user_code`, and `verification_uri`
acceptance_criteria.trigger: a user runs the `signup` or `login` CLI command

acceptance_criteria.action: prints the `verification_uri` and `user_code` to the terminal and instructs the user to visit the URL in a browser
acceptance_criteria.trigger: the CLI receives a `device_code`, `user_code`, and `verification_uri` from the device authorization endpoint

acceptance_criteria.action: polls the token endpoint at the provider-specified interval until authorization is granted or the `device_code` expires
receives a `session token` and persists it to the platform-appropriate `token file` path
acceptance_criteria.trigger: authorization is granted by the user in the browser

acceptance_criteria.action: exits with an informative error message indicating that authorization was not completed in time
acceptance_criteria.trigger: the `device_code` expires before the user completes browser-based authorization

edge_cases.action: must display an error and exit non-zero without overwriting any existing `token file`
edge_cases.error_condition: the device authorization endpoint is unreachable during `signup` or `login`

user_story.goal: have my session token stored securely in a well-known file location with restricted permissions
user_story.benefit: so that other users on the same system cannot read my credentials

acceptance_criteria.action: persists the `session token` as a plain-text file at `~/.config/af-hub/token` on Linux/macOS and `%APPDATA%\af-hub\token` on Windows
creates the `token file` with POSIX permissions `0600` (owner read/write only) on Linux and macOS
creates the `~/.config/af-hub/` directory (or platform equivalent) with permissions `0700` if it does not already exist
restricts the `token file` ACL to the current user only on Windows
reads the `token file` and passes the `session token` as a `bearer token` in the `Authorization` header
acceptance_criteria.trigger: any CLI command that requires authentication is invoked

acceptance_criteria.action: deletes the `token file` and exits successfully
acceptance_criteria.trigger: the user runs the `logout` command and the `token file` exists

acceptance_criteria.action: exits successfully without error
acceptance_criteria.trigger: the user runs the `logout` command and the `token file` does not exist

edge_cases.action: must display an error and exit non-zero without creating a `token file` with insecure permissions
edge_cases.error_condition: the CLI cannot set `0600` permissions on the `token file` due to filesystem limitations

user_story.goal: create a time-bound API key for a repo
user_story.benefit: so that I or an AF instance can authenticate API requests against af-hub for that repo

acceptance_criteria.action: generates a new API key from a minimum of 256 bits (32 random bytes) of cryptographically secure `entropy`, encodes it as `base64url`, stores only the `bcrypt` or `argon2` hash, and returns the plaintext key to the caller exactly once
acceptance_criteria.return_contract: HTTP 201 with the plaintext `base64url`-encoded key and the new `key_id`; the plaintext key is never returned again

acceptance_criteria.action: assigns an explicit `expires_at` timestamp to every API key at creation time
never stores the plaintext API key; only the `bcrypt` or `argon2` hash is persisted
grants the `editor` role (full read/write access) to the `repo` associated with the key to any caller who possesses a valid API key for that repo

edge_cases.action: must reject the request with HTTP 422 and code `validation_error`
edge_cases.error_condition: `POST /v1/keys` is called without a valid `expires_at` date in the request body

user_story.goal: extend the expiry of an existing API key or revoke it
user_story.benefit: so that I can maintain access continuity or immediately cut off access when a key is compromised

acceptance_criteria.action: updates the `expires_at` date on the key and returns HTTP 200
acceptance_criteria.return_contract: HTTP 200 with the updated key metadata
acceptance_criteria.trigger: the key owner or an admin calls `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` with a new `expires_at` value

acceptance_criteria.action: restricts `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` to modifying only the `expires_at` field; all other key attributes are immutable via this endpoint
immediately invalidates the key with no grace period and returns HTTP 200
acceptance_criteria.trigger: the key owner or an admin calls `DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}`

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the request with HTTP 403 and code `permission_denied`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: a caller who is neither the key owner nor an admin attempts `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` or `DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}`

acceptance_criteria.action: must return HTTP 404 and code `key_not_found`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` or `DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}` is called with a `key_id` that does not exist

acceptance_criteria.action: must reject the request with HTTP 422 and code `validation_error`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` is called with fields other than `expires_at` in the request body

edge_cases.action: must immediately reject any subsequent request using the revoked key with HTTP 401 and code `token_invalid`
edge_cases.error_condition: the revoked plaintext key is presented to any endpoint

edge_cases.action: must reject the request with HTTP 422 and code `validation_error` if the new `expires_at` is in the past
edge_cases.error_condition: `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` is called with an `expires_at` date that is earlier than the current server time

user_story.goal: receive a consistent and machine-readable error response for any failed request
user_story.benefit: so that CLI tools and AF instances can programmatically handle errors and implement appropriate retry logic

acceptance_criteria.action: returns all error responses as `application/json` bodies conforming to the schema `{ "error": "<human-readable message>", "code": "<machine-readable code>" }`, even when the success response for the same endpoint uses a different content type

uses only the canonical Phase 1 `code` vocabulary: `token_expired`, `token_invalid`, `permission_denied`, `user_not_found`, `key_not_found`, `repo_not_found`, `conflict`, `validation_error`, `internal_error`
returns HTTP 401 with code `token_expired` for any request presenting a `bearer token` or API key whose `expires_at` timestamp has passed
returns HTTP 401 with code `token_invalid` for any request presenting a malformed, unrecognised, or revoked `bearer token` or API key
returns HTTP 500 with code `internal_error` and logs full error details server-side without exposing internal details in the response body

edge_cases.action: must not introduce any `code` value outside the canonical Phase 1 vocabulary without a PRD amendment
edge_cases.error_condition: any error condition occurs during Phase 1 operation

user_story.goal: have all API endpoints versioned under the /v1/ prefix
user_story.benefit: so that future /v2/ endpoints can be introduced without requiring me to reconfigure base URLs or breaking existing integrations

acceptance_criteria.action: prefixes all Phase 1 API routes with `/v1/`
derives both the `user_id` and the `repo_id` scope exclusively from the `bearer token` presented with each request, without requiring separate query parameters

exposes exactly the following Phase 1 endpoints: `POST /v1/users`, `PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}`, `POST /v1/keys`, `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}`, `DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}`, `POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate`

edge_cases.action: must return HTTP 404 for any request to a path that does not match a defined `/v1/` route
edge_cases.error_condition: a caller requests a path that is not a defined Phase 1 endpoint

user_story.goal: enforce multi-tenancy at the repo level throughout the data model
user_story.benefit: so that data from one repo is never accessible to users scoped to a different repo

acceptance_criteria.action: keys every database table by `repo_id` to enforce the `repo` as the `tenant` boundary
scopes all API calls to a single `repo`, derived from the `bearer token` presented with the request
stores each `(user_id, repo_id)` access grant as a discrete record; a user with access to multiple repos has one record per repo
must return HTTP 404 and code `repo_not_found`
acceptance_criteria.error_condition: a `bearer token` scoped to `repo` A is used to access resources belonging to `repo` B

edge_cases.action: must not expose resources from one `repo` to a `bearer token` scoped to a different `repo`, even if both repos belong to the same user

user_story.goal: have the RBAC model and database schema designed for extensibility
user_story.benefit: so that additional roles and tenant-hierarchy levels can be added in future phases without breaking changes or schema migrations

acceptance_criteria.action: implements exactly one `RBAC` role in Phase 1: `editor`, which grants full read/write access scoped to a `repo`
designs the `RBAC` model and database schema to accommodate additional roles and permission policies in future phases without requiring a schema migration
preserves the machine-readable error code vocabulary as append-only; existing `code` values must not be renamed or removed in future phases

user_story.goal: enforce TLS for all API endpoints in production deployments
user_story.benefit: so that credentials and telemetry data are protected in transit from external users and AF instances

acceptance_criteria.action: must only serve API traffic over TLS
acceptance_criteria.state: the deployment is reachable from the public internet or from external users (production deployment)

acceptance_criteria.action: may serve API traffic over plain HTTP
supports TLS termination at the load balancer or reverse proxy layer (e.g. nginx, AWS ALB); the application itself is not required to terminate TLS directly

correctness_properties.invariant: the plaintext key is derived from a minimum of 256 bits (32 random bytes) of cryptographically secure `entropy`, encoded as `base64url`, and only a `bcrypt` or `argon2` hash of that key is persisted; the plaintext is never stored or returned more than once

correctness_properties.for_any: API key or `bootstrap token` that has passed its `expires_at` timestamp
correctness_properties.invariant: af-hub returns HTTP 401 with code `token_expired` within a single request cycle for every endpoint

correctness_properties.for_any: API key or `bootstrap token` that has been revoked
correctness_properties.invariant: af-hub returns HTTP 401 with code `token_invalid` on the very next request that presents the revoked credential, with no grace period

correctness_properties.for_any: user authenticated via a second `oauth_provider` with an email matching an existing account
correctness_properties.invariant: af-hub links the new provider to the existing account (`auto-merge`) and never creates a duplicate user record
the record carries provenance fields `repo_id`, `created_by`, and `created_at`
both the `user_id` and the `repo_id` scope are derived exclusively from the `bearer token` in the Authorization header; no cross-repo data leakage occurs

correctness_properties.for_any: error response returned by any af-hub endpoint
correctness_properties.invariant: the response body conforms to `{ "error": "<string>", "code": "<canonical-code>" }` and the `code` value is one of the Phase 1 canonical vocabulary entries

correctness_properties.for_any: `token file` written by the af-hub CLI on a POSIX system
correctness_properties.invariant: the file is created with permissions `0600` and its parent directory with permissions `0700`; no other user on the system can read the file

steps.action: Runs af-hub for the first time
Creates admin user and `bootstrap token` with configurable expiry (default 90 days)
Hashes `bootstrap token` with `bcrypt` or `argon2` and stores hash only
Prints plaintext `bootstrap token` to stdout exactly once
Records the printed `bootstrap token` securely for subsequent admin operations
Runs `af-hub signup` CLI command
Calls af-hub device authorization endpoint
Returns `device_code`, `user_code`, and `verification_uri`
Prints `verification_uri` and `user_code` to terminal
Opens `verification_uri` in browser and authenticates with chosen `oauth_provider`
Polls token endpoint at specified interval
Detects authorization grant; provisions new user account (email not previously seen)
Returns `session token` to CLI
Persists `session token` to `token file` with `0600` permissions (POSIX) or restricted ACL (Windows)
Runs `af-hub login` CLI command
Calls af-hub device authorization endpoint
Returns `device_code`, `user_code`, and `verification_uri`
Prints `verification_uri` and `user_code` to terminal
Opens `verification_uri` in browser and authenticates
Polls token endpoint
Resumes existing session; performs `auto-merge` if new `oauth_provider` for same email
Returns `session token` to CLI
Overwrites `token file` with new `session token` and correct permissions
Calls `POST /v1/keys` with valid `bearer token` and desired `expires_at`
Validates token; extracts `user_id` and `repo_id`
Generates 32 cryptographically secure random bytes
Encodes bytes as `base64url` to produce plaintext key
Hashes plaintext key with `bcrypt` or `argon2`; stores hash with `expires_at`, `key_id`, `repo_id`, `created_by`, `created_at`
Returns HTTP 201 with plaintext key and `key_id` (plaintext never stored or returned again)
Calls `DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}` with valid `bearer token`
Validates token; checks caller is key owner or admin
Immediately marks key as revoked with no grace period
Returns HTTP 200 confirming revocation
Attempts to use revoked key on any endpoint
Returns HTTP 401 with code `token_invalid`
Calls `POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate` with current admin `bearer token`
Validates token; confirms admin role
Immediately invalidates old `bootstrap token` with no grace period
Generates new `bootstrap token` and stores only its hash
Returns HTTP 200 with new plaintext `bootstrap token` (returned once, never retrievable again)
Runs `af-hub logout` CLI command
Checks whether `token file` exists at well-known path
Deletes `token file` if present; exits with code 0 regardless

error_handling.condition: Any `bearer token` or API key has passed its `expires_at` timestamp
error_handling.behavior: Return HTTP 401 with JSON body `{ "error": "<message>", "code": "token_expired" }` within the same request cycle

error_handling.condition: Any `bearer token` or API key is malformed, unrecognised, or has been revoked
error_handling.behavior: Return HTTP 401 with JSON body `{ "error": "<message>", "code": "token_invalid" }` within the same request cycle

error_handling.condition: The authenticated principal does not have the required role for the requested operation
The referenced `user_id` does not exist in af-hub
The referenced `key_id` does not exist in af-hub
The referenced `repo` does not exist or is not accessible to the caller's `bearer token`
A `POST /v1/users` request is submitted with an `email` that already exists in the system
A `POST /v1/users` request body omits `username` or `email`, or sets `oauth_provider` without `provider_account_id` (or vice versa)
A `PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}` request body includes fields other than `expires_at`, or supplies an `expires_at` in the past
An unexpected server-side error occurs during request processing
error_handling.behavior: Return HTTP 500 with JSON body `{ "error": "<message>", "code": "internal_error" }`; log full error details server-side; do not expose internal details in the response body
